company.name: Realty Income
company.description: Realty Income is a U.S.-based net lease REIT that owns and manages a large diversified portfolio of commercial properties leased primarily under long-term agreements.

overview.title: Business model
paragraphs: Realty Income operates as a net lease real estate investment trust, acquiring freestanding commercial properties and leasing them to tenants under long-term arrangements where tenants typically bear many property-level operating costs.

The company positions itself around recurring monthly dividends, portfolio diversification, and investment-grade tenant relationships rather than direct operation of the retail businesses occupying its properties.

overview.title: Portfolio exposure
paragraphs: The portfolio is exposed to retail and service categories where property location, lease duration, tenant credit, and capital access drive value.

Because the company owns physical real estate rather than a software platform, its moat comes less from network effects and more from scale, financing, underwriting discipline, lease contracts, and access to acquisition pipelines.

moatNarrative: Realty Income has a durable but not unassailable moat: scale gives it cheaper capital, tenant diversification, public-market credibility, and the ability to transact across a large property base.

The moat is constrained by the replicability of the underlying asset class. Net lease properties can be owned by other REITs, private funds, family offices, cooperatives, or local investors if they can source capital and underwrite tenant risk.
decentralizationNarrative: Realty Income is highly centralized as an ownership and capital-allocation vehicle, but its underlying product is a collection of local physical properties that could theoretically be owned, financed, governed, or energy-retrofitted by more distributed groups.

The strongest decentralization path is not replacing a REIT with software alone; it is unbundling property ownership, financing, energy upgrades, lease transparency, and local stewardship into cooperative or protocol-assisted structures.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 176-200.

moat.rationale: Scale, public REIT status, capital access, tenant diversification, and long operating history support a strong net lease moat, but the owned properties and leases are not technically unique and can be competed for by other capital pools.

decentralizability.rationale: The centralized REIT vehicle is hard to decentralize directly, but the underlying assets are local and separable enough to support cooperative ownership, tokenized governance, shared retrofit finance, or open property-performance reporting.

profitability.rationale: Realty Income reports REIT operating results built around recurring rental revenue and funds-from-operations style performance, though GAAP net income is affected by depreciation, acquisitions, interest expense, and real estate accounting.

peRatio.rationale: A conventional P/E ratio is a weak valuation lens for REITs because depreciation and real estate accounting can distort GAAP earnings; this score treats headline earnings valuation as speculative and secondary to cash-flow metrics.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap listed Realty Income near the high tens of billions of dollars in market capitalization around the source review window; the value is rounded for registry scoring rather than treated as an intraday quote.

products.name: Net lease real estate portfolio
products.summary: A diversified portfolio of commercial properties leased to tenants under long-term net lease structures.
products.whyItMatters: Net lease REITs concentrate ownership, underwriting, and capital access for everyday commercial real estate, shaping who captures local property cash flows and who decides how those properties are financed and upgraded.
replacementSketch: A credible replacement would not simply copy Realty Income property by property. It would make smaller-scale commercial property ownership easier to syndicate, govern, audit, and refinance without depending on one public REIT balance sheet.

The most plausible path combines cooperative property ownership, transparent lease-performance reporting, and standardized retrofit finance so local investors, tenants, and communities can coordinate around durable cash-flowing assets.

alternatives.name: Open REIT data cooperative
alternatives.summary: A cooperative research and ownership model that uses public filings, local records, member governance, and shared underwriting templates to coordinate smaller commercial real estate ownership pools.

openness.rationale: The model can be built from public filings, open analytical templates, and member-visible governance, but many lease details, appraisals, and financing terms remain private or jurisdiction-specific.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Cooperative ownership and locally governed investment pools directly reduce reliance on a single centralized REIT manager, though professional underwriting and compliance remain necessary.

readiness.rationale: The ingredients exist, including public filings and cooperative governance models, but a mature open alternative to public net lease REIT scale is not yet established.

costLeverage.rationale: Shared diligence, standardized reporting, and pooled local capital could reduce fees and improve alignment, but large REITs still have meaningful financing and transaction-cost advantages.

disruptionConcepts.name: Cooperative net lease ownership network
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federated cooperative ownership model could let local investors, tenants, and community institutions pool capital into smaller commercial properties while sharing open underwriting templates, lease-performance reporting, and governance rules across regions.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market structure shifts from a centralized public REIT acquiring and managing properties at scale toward a network of locally accountable ownership pools using common standards for diligence, reporting, and governance.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federated ownership and governance rather than through Bitcoin as the core primitive. Bitcoin or Lightning could be used at the margin for low-friction member distributions or escrow, but the essential mechanism is cooperative capital formation and transparent property reporting.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Property sponsors list opportunities with standardized lease, tenant, location, financing, and maintenance data; members vote on acquisitions and budgets; regional operators handle property administration under auditable service agreements.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Public filings, county property records, bank statements, lease attestations, third-party inspections, and member-visible operating reports constrain false reporting. Larger acquisitions would still need licensed legal, accounting, and appraisal review.
failureModes: Local investor pools may lack the capital access and underwriting discipline that gives large REITs an advantage.
Private lease terms, tenant credit risk, securities-law compliance, and property-management quality can still centralize practical control in professional managers.
adoptionPath: Start with small single-tenant commercial properties where public records and lease economics are simple enough for standardized diligence.
Create federated reporting standards so independent cooperatives can compare performance, share templates, and eventually co-invest across regions.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept directly decentralizes ownership and governance of commercial property cash flows, although regulated finance and property administration prevent full peer-to-peer autonomy.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Shared underwriting standards and cooperative governance are credible coordination tools, but sourcing, financing, and compliance remain operationally demanding.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The model is feasible for small portfolios using existing legal structures and public data, but difficult to scale to Realty Income's breadth without professionalized management.

incumbentPressure.rationale: It could pressure management fees, transparency expectations, and local ownership narratives, but it is unlikely to displace large REIT capital advantages quickly.

disruptionConcepts.name: Tenant-site energy retrofit cooperatives
disruptionConcepts.summary: A distributed energy retrofit cooperative could finance solar, storage, metering, and efficiency upgrades across leased commercial sites, letting tenants, landlords, and local capital pools share savings without depending entirely on a centralized REIT's capital plan.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The competitive pressure comes from turning commercial properties into locally financed energy assets, shifting part of the value proposition from rent collection to open, measurable, site-level energy productivity.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Bitcoin is not central here. Decentralization matters through open energy hardware, site-level generation, cooperative retrofit finance, and auditable metering that can coordinate many small properties without one landlord controlling the whole upgrade stack.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Tenants, landlords, installers, and local investors coordinate around standardized retrofit contracts, shared savings formulas, open metering data, and pooled procurement for solar, storage, and controls.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Metered production, utility bills, equipment telemetry, inspection records, and agreed baselines constrain inflated savings claims. Cheating risks remain where baselines are manipulated, equipment is poorly maintained, or tenants change usage patterns.
failureModes: Lease structures may misalign incentives when landlords pay for upgrades but tenants capture utility savings, or vice versa.
Permitting, interconnection delays, roof conditions, tenant turnover, and financing costs can erase projected retrofit economics.
adoptionPath: Pilot on cooperative-owned or landlord-aligned retail sites where utility savings and roof rights are easy to document.
Publish open retrofit performance datasets and contract templates so independent property pools can replicate the model.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Distributed generation, open metering, and cooperative finance can decentralize a meaningful layer of property value even if land ownership remains partly centralized.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Shared savings contracts and metered output are credible, but multi-party landlord-tenant-investor coordination is structurally difficult.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Commercial solar, efficiency, and metering primitives are mature, but packaging them into a replicable cooperative retrofit product across leased retail sites remains challenging.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept pressures landlords to expose energy data and share retrofit economics, but it is more likely to complement or modestly discipline net lease REITs than replace them outright.

summaryMarkdown: Prepared a new Realty Income registry bundle with one core product, one cooperative ownership alternative, and two disruption concepts: federated cooperative net lease ownership and tenant-site distributed energy retrofit cooperatives.
